original_abstract: In recent years, scholars have discovered that the American public responds to foreign policy issues on the basis of fairly stable broad orientations toward international affairs, influenced by a number of demographic, ideological, and partisan factors. Although there has been much recent speculation about the role that religion plays in shaping such orientations, there are very few empirical analyses of that influence. In this article, I use the 2012 Chicago Council on Global Affairs survey to classify American religious groups on Wittkopf s (1990) classic dimensions of foreign policy attitudes: militant internationalism and cooperative internationalism. I find rather different religious constituencies for each perspective, with Evangelical Protestants and religious traditionalists from other faiths most supportive of militant internationalism, while ethnoreligious minorities and religious modernists are most likely to back cooperative internationalism.
humanized_abstract: In recent years scholars have identified a set of relatively stable “public moods” with regard to foreign policy issues, which are shaped by a variety of different variables, including demographic, ideological and partisan. There has been a good deal of discussion of late as to whether religion plays a significant part in this process, but there has been very little empirical research about how this might occur. The present study makes use of the 2012 survey from the Chicago Council on Global Affairs to examine foreign policy attitudes among different religious groups, using Wittkopf's (1990) militant and cooperative dimensions as the framework for analysis. The results show that somewhat different religious constituencies are associated with each foreign policy perspective. Evangelical Protestants and other religious traditionalists from outside of the Protestant tradition are particularly prone to militant internationalism, while ethnoreligious minorities and those from more modernist religious persuasions are more likely to be associated with cooperative internationalism.

The present study makes use of the 2012 survey from the Chicago Council on Global Affairs to examine foreign policy attitudes among different religious groups, using Wittkopf's (1990) militant and cooperative dimensions as the framework for analysis. The results show that somewhat different religious constituencies are associated with each foreign policy perspective. Evangelical Protestants and other religious traditionalists from outside of the Protestant tradition are particularly prone to militant internationalism, while ethnoreligious minorities and those from more modernist religious persuasions are more likely to be associated with cooperative internationalism.
